turns.content: I'm trying to understand why my trait method is getting shadowed by an inherent method when I use a generic struct with Deref traits.

That's a classic Rust gotcha involving the method resolution order. When you have a type that implements Deref, the compiler performs auto-ref and auto-deref steps before looking up methods. It will prefer inherent methods on the actual concrete type over trait methods from traits like Iterator or Debug unless you explicitly disambiguate.
So if I define a trait method in my generic struct and also implement Deref to another type, which one gets called?

The inherent methods on the concrete type win first. The compiler traverses the deref chain looking for an exact match before considering trait implementations. If your struct has an inherent method with the same name, it shadows any trait method unless you use a qualified path like self::TraitName::method.
Can you show me how this plays out in my code with std::collections::HashMap?

Consider a scenario where you have a struct that Deref to HashMap. If you define an inherent method called iter() on your struct, calling it will invoke the inherent method rather than the trait method from std::collections::HashMap. The deref chain lookup happens before trait resolution.
I see, but what if I want both to be available? How do I handle the conflict?

You can resolve this by using a qualified path in your trait method definition. Instead of defining self::trait_method(), you define self::TraitName::method() where TraitName is the specific type being derefed to, ensuring it doesn't conflict with inherent methods.
Does this apply to all traits or just some specific ones?

This applies universally across std traits like Iterator, Debug, and Clone. The core principle remains: inherent methods take precedence in the resolution order unless you explicitly qualify them with a trait path.
What about when I have multiple Deref targets in my type hierarchy?

The deref chain is built from the first target and then recursively to subsequent ones. You can use DerefMut for mutable operations, but you still follow the same resolution rules. The compiler will check each link in the chain until it finds a method or exhausts the list.
I'm confused about how auto-ref interacts with this.

Auto-ref handles references automatically, but in your case, you're dealing with deref chains. The compiler treats the type as if it were its deref target when resolving methods, then checks inherent vs trait methods on that resolved type.
Okay, so I need to be careful about naming my trait methods?
Exactly. To avoid shadowing issues, you should either use qualified paths in your trait implementations or ensure your inherent method names are distinct from common trait methods.
What if I want to override a standard library method like iter()?
You can do that by implementing the trait on your type instead of relying on Deref. Or you can use qualified paths in your inherent methods to explicitly reference the trait implementation if needed.
Wait, so I should avoid inherent methods with the same name as common traits?
Correct. The safest approach is to either use qualified paths or ensure your method names don't conflict with standard trait methods like iter(), next(), or clone().
Ah, that makes sense now. I was trying to implement a custom iterator but it wasn't working because of the shadowing.

That's exactly why you needed to qualify your trait method path when defining inherent methods on types that deref to standard collections. Now your custom iteration logic will work as expected without being obscured by the HashMap's built-in iter().
